details.text: Derivative: بَرَاءَةٌ (Baraa’ah) comes from the verb بَرِئَ – يَبرَأ – بَرَاءَةٌ means to be free of something like an illness or association. Here the meaning is a declaration of “dissociation”.

Derivative: مَرَدُوا comes from مَرَدَ – يَمْرُدُ – مُرُود which means to be recalcitrant and defiant i.e., obstinate in rebelliousness. مَرَدَ with عَلَى means to become accustomed to something and continue upon it.

Derivative: مُرْجَوْنَ comes from the verb أَرْجَى – يُرْجِي – إِِرْجَاء which means to delay and to defer something from its time. مُرْجُونَ (Murjoona) refers to those who have been deferred and made to wait.
Derivative: جُرُفٍ is a bank or ledge of something like the last part of a cliff before the fall or riverbank before the water underneath.

Derivative: هَارٍ is the active participle from هَارَ – يَهُورُ – هَوْر which means to topple, demolish or bring down. هَارٍ here indicates to the meaning of a ledge or bank that is collapsing and crashing down.
Derivative: انْهَارَ comes in origin from هَارَ and means to be demolished or to collapse. It is usually used to describe buildings or built structures like walls collapsing and being brought down.
Derivative: تَقَطَّعَ refers to an extreme form of cutting and means “to cut to pieces”.

Derivative: بَايَعْتُم is from بَايَعَ – يُبَايِعُ – مُبَايَعَة means to make a contract between two parties and is mutual. It differs from بَاعَ – يَبِيعُ – بِيْعٌ which means “to sell” and is one way.

Derivative: السَّائِحُونَ is the plural of سَائِح (Saa’ih) which is the active participle from سَاحَ - يَسِيحُ which means “to travel”. السَّائِحُونَ here can therefore refer to travellers for the sake of Allah. It has also been said to refer to those who fast as سَائِح (Saa’ih) is also used in Arabic to refer to the صَائِم (Saa’im) “Fasting person” in the case where the traveller gives is deprived of things that he normally has access to like the one who fasts.

التَّائِبُونَ الْعَابِدُونَ الْحَامِدُونَ السَّائِحُونَ الرَّاكِعُونَ السَّاجِدُونَ الْآمِرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَالنَّاهُونَ عَنِ الْمُنكَرِ وَالْحَافِظُونَ لِحُدُودِ اللَّهِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ

- The use of nouns which implies permanency in respect to the act instead of verbs which imply temporariness implies that they are not just undertaking these acts but are constantly engaged in them or are committed to them.

Derivative: نَّكَثُوا is from نَكَثَ which is one of the three words used for violating and breaking an oath, covenant or agreement. The other two are حَنِثَ and نَقَضَ. نَكَثَ (Nakatha) is the weakest in implication out of the three and نَقَضَ (Naqada) is the strongest.

Derivative: طَعَنُوا comes from طَعَنَ – يَطْعَنُ – طَعْن and in origin means to stab and poke at something or somebody with something sharp. It is used figuratively to imply the meaning of saying hurtful things that really bite. It can include sharp criticism, degrading speech and defamation.

Derivative: حَرِيصٌ (Harees) refers to someone who is “greedy and covetous” over something which is very important to him and implies eagerness and zealousness. Its meaning here could be understood to be “deeply concerned”.
- “And He will cure (وَيَشْفِ) the chests (صُدُورَ)” – It means “what is inside the chests” i.e., in terms of feelings and emotions.

Derivative: وَلِيجَةً (Waleejah) comes from وَلَجَ – يَلِجُ – وُلُوجًا which means to enter into something. وَلِيجَةً (Waleejah) refers to someone who is an intimate friend or confidant because he has entered and is included within your intimate and closely held affairs and matters.

Derivative: سِيحُوا is the plural command form from the verb سَاحَ – يَسِيحُ – سَيْحًا which in origin refers to the flowing or trickling of water and its spreading out according to its natural course and was then used to refer to travelling through the land freely.
Derivative: تَرَبَّصُوا is the plural command form from the verb تَرَبَّصَ which means “to wait” and implies lying in wait for something to happen or to befall you.

Derivative: مُعْجِزِي is from أَعْجَزَ – يُعْجِزُ – إِعْجَاز which means to incapacitate. مُعْجِز (Mu’jiz) is the one who weakens or incapacitates somebody or something else by taking away its capability. The meaning here in His statement غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللَّهِ implies that you will not be able to prevent or escape from Allah’s power.
Derivative: يُضَاهِئُونَ comes from دَاهَى – يُدَاهِي – مُدَاهَاة which means to copy and imitate.
Derivative: رُهْبَان (Ruhbaan) is the plural of رَاهِب (Raahib) and refers to the Christian ascetics who would dedicate themselves to worship and isolate from worldly life in monasteries i.e., monks.
Derivative: أَحْبَارَ (Ahbaar) is the plural of حَبْر or حِبْر (Habr/Hibr) and refers to the Jewish scholars.
Derivative: يَأْبَى comes from أَبَى – يَأْبَى – اِبَاء means “to refuse”. With إِلَّا (Illaa – Except) following it, أَبَى means “to refuse any alternative”.

Derivative: النَّسِيءُ (An-Nasee’u) means “deferment” and “to delay from its time”. It refers to a practise of the Quraish where they would defer the sacred months and exchange them for others for a benefit of theirs like to engage in warfare which was forbidden during those four sacred months.
Derivative: يُوَاطِئُوا comes from وَاطَأَ – يُوَاطِئُ – مُوَاطَأةً which means “to agree, conform and correspond” with something.
Derivative: انفِرُوا is from نَفَرَ – يَنْفِرُ which means to go forth and is used in respect to an army going out for battle.

Derivative: اثَّاقَلْتُمْ is in origin from تَثَاقَلَ but has become مُدْغَم (Mudgham) which is when a letter transforms into another letter which becomes doubled. Here the تَاء (Taa’) becomes a ثَاء (Thaa’) resulting in two Thaa’s. تَثَاقَلَ means to become heavy and weighted down. It is used here to refer to becoming attached to the ground in the case where someone doesn’t want to move from where he is.
- مَا with لِ plus an attached pronoun is an expression that means: “What’s the matter with such and such person?” or “What’s wrong with such and such person?”.
Derivative: يُظَاهِرُوا is from ظَاهَرَ – مُظَاهَرَة / ظِهَار and means to support some against others.

Derivative: قَاصِدًا (Qaasid) comes from the verb قَصَدَ (Qasada) which carries the meaning of adopting a middle or balanced path and to be economical. قَاصِدًا (Qaasid) here refers to something that is moderate and not over burdensome.

Derivative: عَرَضًا (‘Arad) refers to that which is presented (عَرَضَ – ‘Arada) to a person from the benefits and delights of the Dunyaa. Here it refers to the prize of booty. عَرَض (‘Arad) can also refer to something that is fleeting and doesn’t last long i.e., benefits that are immediate or short-term.
Derivative: الشُّقَّةُ (Shuqqah) refers to a distance that is not covered in travel except through enduring a great deal of difficultly.
Derivative: يَتَرَدَّدُونَ comes from تَرَدَّدَ – تَرَدُّد and means to go back and forth and to shift repeatedly. It is used to refer to hesitation and wavering between one position and another.

Derivative: الْقَاعِدِينَ is the plural of قَاعِد (Qaa’id) which refers to the one who sits for a long period of time. It is used like the word خَوالِفُ (Khawaalif) to refer to those who stay behind from battle and its use here in relation to men carries a negative connotation in the sense that it places them alongside women, children, elderly and those incapable of going out for battle.
Derivative: ثَبَّطَ – تَثْبِيط (Tathbeet) means to hold back and prevent. It is used to refer to repelling a person from doing something that he wants by handicapping, obstructing or preventing him.
Derivative: سَمَّاعُونَ is the plural of سَمَّاع (Sammaa’) which refers to someone who “listens attentively or eagerly” to someone else.

Derivative: أَوْضَعُوا comes from أَوْضَعَ – يُوضِعُ – إِيضاعًا which was used to refer to the act of applying pressure down on the neck of a horse to make it ride faster and is generally used to refer to moving fast. Here أَوْضَعُوا means that “they would have hurried or scurried about” amongst you i.e., in your ranks.

Derivative: خَبَالًا (Khabaal) comes from خَبَلَ – يَخْبُلُ – خَبْل. The origin of خَبَالًا (Khabaal) is a disturbance or sickness that effects the mind and makes it not function, like grief can cause خَبَالًا (Khabaal) i.e., make the mind or reason stop functioning properly. It is used here to imply a source of instability and disturbance that will stop you from functioning properly.
Derivative: انسَلَخَ in relation to a period of time it means to pass or end.
Derivative: مَرْصَدٍ comes from رَصَدَ which means to lie in wait and to watch. مَرْصَدٍ (Marsad) is a place where you watch and lie in wait and is used to refer to a place of ambush.
Derivative: خَلُّوا is the command form from the verb خَلَّى – يُخَلِّي - تَخْلِيَة which means to leave something or someone alone.

Derivative: تُصِبْ comes from أَصَابَ – يُصِيبُ – إِصَابَة and in origin means to hit a target and was used in respect to an arrow hitting its target. It is used to mean to befall or be struck by something but keeps the implication of hitting the target in the sense that it was meant to hit or befall you. مُصِيبَة (Museebah) which is derived from it however is used to refer to something very bad that has struck like some kind of calamity.
Derivative: تَرَبَّصُونَ comes from تَرَبَّصَ which means to wait for the opportune moment to do something or to lie in wait watching for that opportunity or timing.

Derivative: تَزْهَقَ comes from زَهَقَ – يَزْهَقُ – زَهْق / زُهُوق. زَهَقَ is used when something perishes and disappears or diminishes to nothing. زُهُوقُ النَّفْس (Zuhooqu n-Nafs) is used to refer to the exiting of the soul from the body as a reference to death.
Derivative: يَفْرَقُونَ comes from فَرِقَ “Fariqa” (not to be confused with فَرَقَ “Faraqa” which means to separate, divide or differ). فَرِقَ – يَفْرَقُ – فَرَق means to be scared and terrified.
Derivative: يَحْلِفُونَ comes from حَلَفَ – يَحْلِفُ – حِلْف / حَلْف and is one of the words used to mean “to swear an oath”. It is used specifically when an oath is sworn in order to make amends.
Derivative: يَجْمَحُونَ comes from جَمَحَ – يَجْمَحُ – جُمُوح / جِمَاح and refers to a horse breaking from its constraints and bolting. It implies moving quickly and not under control.
Derivative: يَلْمِزُ is from لَمَزَ which carries the meaning of to find fault or blame in somebody i.e., to criticise them.
Derivative: مَأْمَنَ (Ma’man) is from أَمِنَ - يَأْمَنُ – أَمْنًا which means to be safe and secure. مَأْمَن (Ma’man) is the place where someone is secure and safe.

Derivative: اسْتَجَارَ means to ask for protection or a promise of security. It comes from جِوَار which is protection or security that is offered to someone which in origin comes from the word جَار which is “a neighbour” because it was customary to protect one’s neighbour.

- “And the bringing together or reconciling (وَالْمُؤَلَّفَةِ) of their hearts (قُلُوبُهُمْ)” – This represents a special construct in Arabic known as النَّعْتُ السَّبَبِي (An-Na’at As-Sababiy) which is when the نَعْت (Na’t- Adjective) explains the description of a matter connected with the matter being described (المَنْعُوت – Al-Man’oot). It is characterised by the adjective coming before that which it is describing.
- الْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ “Al-Mu’allafati Quloobuhum” refers to those whom it is hoped will accept Islam or make their Islam firmer. There are other explanations regarding whom the term covers but in general it relates to giving Sadaqah to certain people through which it is hoped that Islam will spread further and become more strongly consolidated.

- “He believes in Allah (يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ) and he concedes (وَيُؤْمِنُ) to the believers (لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ)” – Observe here the difference between يُؤْمِنُ بِ (Yu’minu Bi) and يُؤْمِنُ لِ (Yu’minu Li). The former implies “belief” in something and the latter implies “giving in” to something. Here the giving in implies that he ﷺ listens to the believers i.e., his ear is open to their concerns and he listens with good intent towards them.

Derivative: نَخُوضُ comes from خَاضَ which means to plunge or rush into something. It is used to imply plunging or engaging in something without thought and care. With speech it implies engaging in thoughtless pointless speech or jumping into conversations just for the sake of it.

Derivative: نَسُوا is from نَسِيَ which means “to forget” however the meaning here is not to forget something in the sense of no longer remembering it but rather in the sense of ignoring and casting concern or care away from someone or something.

Derivative: خَلَاقِ (Khalaaq) means a “portion” or “share” of something. It comes from خَلْق (Khalq) which means “creation” and can imply the meaning of “a portion from creation”. Here it refers to their “portion” from the life of this world (Dunyaa).

Derivative: الْمُؤْتَفِكَاتِ which comes from إِفْك (Ifk) which carries the meaning of “to turn something round or upside down”. اِئْتِفَاك means to turn something over by making its top its bottom i.e., turning upside down and destroyed. الْمُؤْتَفِكَاتِ refers to the towns which were turned upside down and destroyed and is said to refer to the nation of Lut (as).
Derivative: طَيِّبَةً is the feminine of طَيِّب (Tayyib) which refers to something that is good, wholesome and pure.

Derivative: اغْلُظْ is the command form from غَلُظَ – يَغْلُظُ – غِلَاظ and means to be tough. In relation to dealing with someone غِلَاظ (Ghilaazh) means to be hard and harsh i.e., not to show kindness or softness.

Derivative: إِلًّا refers to those expectations or responsibilities associated with close relationships like for example the expectations and responsibilities that exists between a father and his son because of that relationship.

Derivative: يَرْقُبُوا comes from رَقَبَ – يَرْقَبُ – رِقَابَة which means to watch and observe. It is used to mean to take something into consideration, to observe or respect it i.e., like an agreement.

- “They will not observe (لَا يَرْقُبُوا) in respect to you (فِيكُمْ) any responsibility of relationship (إِلًّا) nor covenant of protection (وَلَا ذِمَّةً)” – This is an expression to mean that given the chance they will treat you ruthlessly.
details.arabic_part: لَا يَرْقُبُوا فِيكُمْ إِلًّا وَلَا ذِمَّةً

details.text: Derivative: الطَّوْلِ (At-Tawl) refers to financial means and a great deal of wealth i.e., deep pockets.

Derivative: الدَّوَائِرَ (Dawaa’ir) is the plural of دَائِرَة (Daa’irah) which in origin means “a circle” and is used to refer to the alternating of time or turn of events which sometime bring fortune and sometimes misfortune. It is also used to refer to misfortunes and adversities that come in general.
